stocks.name: ZoomInfo Technologies
stocks.explanation: ZoomInfo (which has begun trading under the ticker GTM in 2026) is a small-cap sales-and-marketing intelligence software vendor, and the outsized single-day print reflected thin, low-volume trading rather than a confirmed corporate catalyst. Fundamentally the company has been growing revenue only in the low single digits while expanding its base of large enterprise accounts. As a low-priced, high-beta software name it is prone to sharp percentage swings on modest order flow. Treat the magnitude of this move with caution given the light volume behind it.

stocks.name: AppLovin Corporation
stocks.explanation: AppLovin extended a rough stretch, having already fallen roughly 16% in June and more than 20% year-to-date despite broadly bullish analyst coverage. Pressure has come from software-sector headwinds, notable insider selling by executives including the CEO, and execution risk around the open, self-serve rollout of its AXON AI ad-targeting engine. Twenty-nine of 32 analysts still rate the stock a buy with price targets well above current levels, framing the decline as sentiment-driven rather than a fundamental break. The name remains one of the most volatile large-cap ad-tech stocks.

stocks.name: Arm Holdings
stocks.explanation: Arm Holdings sold off alongside the broader semiconductor complex as Wall Street rotated out of AI and chip names on renewed doubts about the payoff from massive AI capex. As a high-multiple licensing and IP company, Arm carries elevated beta to swings in AI sentiment. Weakness in memory peers after SK Hynix’s cautious outlook amplified the risk-off tone across the chip sector. No company-specific catalyst drove the decline.

stocks.name: IonQ Inc.
stocks.explanation: IonQ fell as the speculative quantum-computing rally reversed, with pre-profit quantum names trading almost entirely on theme, sentiment, and headline flow. Weakness in AI and chip stocks spilled into high-beta corners of the market, dragging the group lower. These stocks routinely swing 10-15% on little or no company-specific news, and IonQ had rallied sharply in prior sessions. The move looks like profit-taking after a hot run rather than a change in fundamentals.

stocks.name: Marvell Technology
stocks.explanation: Marvell Technology dropped with the semiconductor group as investors pared exposure to AI-infrastructure chip suppliers. The stock is closely tied to demand for custom AI silicon and data-center connectivity, making it sensitive to shifts in AI-spending sentiment. SK Hynix’s weak memory outlook and a broader chip selloff set a risk-off backdrop. There was no Marvell-specific news behind the decline.

stocks.name: Western Digital
stocks.explanation: Western Digital slid as SK Hynix’s disappointing outlook rattled the memory sector, pushing memory-related stocks toward bear-market territory. Storage and memory names are highly cyclical and move together on pricing and demand signals from the major Asian suppliers. The selloff followed earlier July volatility sparked by Samsung earnings. Concerns about a potential supply glut weighed on sentiment across the group.

stocks.name: Rigetti Computing
stocks.explanation: Rigetti Computing declined as the quantum-computing trade cooled and high-beta speculative names came under pressure. Like its quantum peers, Rigetti is largely pre-revenue and trades on sentiment and headline momentum rather than fundamentals. Weakness in AI and chip stocks bled into the group after a strong prior session. Double-digit intraday swings are routine for the stock.

stocks.name: Atlassian Corporation
stocks.explanation: Atlassian gained even as enterprise software faced sector-wide pressure, with the stock supported by a recent Gartner recognition that helped offset an earlier analyst price-target cut. The collaboration-software maker benefits from steady seat expansion and its push into AI-assisted developer and IT workflows. On a down day for much of software, the relative strength stood out. The move reflected company-specific positives against a weak sector tape.

stocks.name: Seagate Technology
stocks.explanation: Seagate Technology fell alongside Western Digital and Micron as the memory and storage complex sold off on SK Hynix’s cautious outlook. Hard-disk and storage names are cyclical and trade closely with memory-pricing sentiment. Renewed supply-glut fears drove the group lower after earlier-month volatility. No Seagate-specific catalyst was behind the drop.

stocks.name: Pure Storage
stocks.explanation: Pure Storage rose against a weak storage-sector backdrop, with the enterprise flash-storage vendor benefiting from its positioning in AI-driven data infrastructure. Unlike commodity memory makers, Pure sells higher-margin all-flash systems and subscription services less exposed to memory-pricing cycles. That differentiation helped it decouple from the memory selloff. The gain underscored investor preference for storage names with AI-infrastructure exposure.

stocks.name: Micron Technology
stocks.explanation: Micron Technology dropped as SK Hynix’s weak outlook dragged the memory group toward a bear market, reviving fears of a supply glut. As the largest US memory maker, Micron is a bellwether for DRAM and NAND pricing and moves sharply on peer commentary. The decline extended volatility that began earlier in July around Samsung’s earnings. Sentiment, not a Micron-specific event, drove the move.

stocks.name: Lam Research
stocks.explanation: Lam Research fell with the semiconductor-equipment group as the memory selloff and broader chip weakness weighed on capital-equipment names. Lam derives a large share of revenue from memory-chip manufacturers, so a softer memory outlook directly pressures its demand narrative. SK Hynix’s cautious guidance set the tone for the sector. There was no Lam-specific catalyst.

stocks.name: Quantum Computing Inc.
stocks.explanation: Quantum Computing Inc. slid as the quantum-stock rally reversed and speculative, pre-profit names sold off across the board. The stock trades almost purely on sentiment and headline flow, with double-digit daily swings common. Weakness in AI and chip stocks spilled into the group after a strong prior session. The decline reflected profit-taking rather than any change in the company’s prospects.

stocks.name: Infosys Limited
stocks.explanation: Infosys, the India-based IT services and consulting giant, rose modestly ahead of and around the start of the sector’s quarterly earnings season. The ADR is lower-priced and can move on currency shifts, deal-pipeline commentary, and broad IT-services sentiment. Demand for AI-enablement and digital-transformation services has been a supportive theme for the large outsourcers. The gain was modest and consistent with steady, defensive IT-services positioning.

stocks.name: Intel Corporation
stocks.explanation: Intel declined with the broader chip complex as investors rotated out of semiconductors on AI-capex-return doubts and the memory selloff. The move came even as Intel announced a €5 billion expansion of its Irish hub, underscoring that macro sector sentiment outweighed the company-specific investment news. Heavy trading volume accompanied the drop. Intel remains highly sensitive to swings in overall chip-sector risk appetite.
